title: End of module: Regression and Gradient Descent

questions.text: For the sales data $(1,2), (2,5), (3,8)$, the candidate line $\hat{y} = 2x$ (i.e. $w=2$, $b=0$) gives residuals $0, -1, -2$. Compute its mean squared error.
For the same data $(1,2), (2,5), (3,8)$, compute the mean squared error of the line $\hat{y} = 3x - 1$.
questions.explanation: $\hat{y} = 3x-1$ gives predictions $2, 5, 8$ — exactly matching every $y$, so every residual is $0$ and $\text{MSE} = 0$.

questions.text: Why does the mean squared error square each residual instead of just averaging the residuals directly?
choices: So positive and negative errors can't cancel out, and larger errors are penalised more heavily
Because squaring is computationally faster than addition
So that a very good fit can have a negative loss
Because $x$ must always be squared before comparing to $y$
questions.explanation: Averaging signed residuals directly would let a line that overshoots high on some points and low on others look perfect on average. Squaring makes every contribution positive and grows faster for larger errors.

questions.text: For the sales data $(1,2),(2,5),(3,8)$ starting from $w=0$, $b=0$, compute $\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial w} = \frac{2}{n}\sum_i (wx_i+b-y_i)x_i$.
Using the same starting point $w=0$, $b=0$, compute $\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial b} = \frac{2}{n}\sum_i (wx_i+b-y_i)$.

Using learning rate $\eta = 0.01$ and the gradient $\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial w} = -24$ from $w=0$, compute the updated weight $w \leftarrow w - \eta\,\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial w}$.

Using the same learning rate $\eta = 0.01$ and the gradient $\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial b} = -10$ from $b=0$, compute the updated bias $b \leftarrow b - \eta\,\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial b}$.
Starting instead from the flat guess $w=0$, $b=1$ (so $\hat{y}=1$ for every $x$) on the same data, compute $\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial b}$.
Using learning rate $\eta = 0.1$ and the gradient $\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial b} = -8$ from Q8, compute the updated bias starting from $b=1$.
questions.explanation: $b \leftarrow 1 - (0.1)(-8) = 1 + 0.8 = 1.8$. Note this moves $b$ further from the true intercept $-1$ — a single step does not always move every parameter closer to its final value.

questions.text: What typically happens if the learning rate in gradient descent is set far too large?
choices: Training stops immediately and the weights freeze
Each step can overshoot the minimum, and the loss may oscillate or diverge instead of decreasing
The model instantly reaches the exact global minimum with no downside
It has no effect on training, only on the final rounding of the answer
questions.explanation: A learning rate that is too large causes each update to overshoot the bottom of the loss bowl; repeated overshooting can make the loss grow without bound (divergence) rather than shrink.

questions.text: What typically happens if the learning rate is set far too small?
choices: Training converges correctly but needs far more epochs to get close to the minimum
questions.explanation: A tiny learning rate takes tiny steps: each epoch reduces the loss only slightly, so reaching a good fit requires many more epochs than a well-chosen learning rate.

questions.text: Which of the following are true of batch gradient descent applied to this 1-D linear regression's MSE loss?
choices: Each epoch computes the gradient using every point in the training data
The MSE loss, as a function of $w$ and $b$, is convex, so with a small enough learning rate gradient descent converges to the global minimum
A single epoch is always enough to reach the best-fitting line exactly
Each update moves $w$ and $b$ in the direction that most increases the loss
questions.explanation: Batch gradient descent sums over the whole dataset every epoch (true), and the MSE surface for linear regression is a convex bowl, so a suitably small learning rate is guaranteed to converge to the global minimum (true). One epoch is rarely enough (false — see the worked trace, where two epochs only made partial progress), and updates move *against* the gradient to *decrease* the loss, not increase it (false).

questions.text: What is the name of the algorithm that repeatedly nudges $w$ and $b$ against the gradient of the loss, a small step at a time, to minimise it?
questions.explanation: This is **gradient descent** (here, batch gradient descent, since every step uses the whole dataset): $w \leftarrow w - \eta\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial w}$ and $b \leftarrow b - \eta\frac{\partial \text{MSE}}{\partial b}$.
